coredata.dc:title: Rapid and sensitive screening of multiple polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons by a reusable fluorescent sensor array
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Linfeng
Tian, Xike
Li, Yong
Yang, Chao
Huang, Yunjie
Nie, Yulun
coredata.dc:description: Simple and rapid sensing of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) remains a great technical challenge due to their chemical stability and structural similarity. Here, a simple, sensitive and cost-effective sensing strategy is proposed to detect multiple PAHs by utilizing the inner filter effect (IFE) and a reusable fluorescent sensor array consisting of four polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite carbon quantum dots (CDs) film sensors. The CDs/PVA films have a wide and tunable excitation range, which provide sufficient spectral overlap with PAHs and ensure the efficient occurrence of IFE. Under different excitations, the diverse UV absorption capacities of PAHs resulted in diverse spectral responses, enabling a unique chemical fingerprint for each PAH. Upon multivariate pattern recognition analysis, the array rendered high-throughput discrimination and sensitive quantification of 16 priority PAHs with 100% classification accuracy and detection limit as low as 57 nM. Moreover, the rapid and accurate screening of multiple environmental samples were also realized with the results consistent with high-performance liquid chromatography. This IFE-based reusable array is readily prepared, green and feasible, which exhibits great potential in environmental analysis and brings an advanced strategy to high-throughput sensing of more pollutants with similar structures and lack of recognition sites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons
Reusable array
PAHs screening
Fluorescent sensor
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Diversified response of film sensors to PAHs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The structures of 16 priority PAHs listed by the EPA and (b) the corresponding normalized UV–vis absorption spectra in DMF solution (10 µM).

(a) Synthetic routes of four CDs. (b) Photographs of B-CDs, C-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs dispersed in DMF in daylight (left), and under λ = 365 nm UV irradiation (right). (c) The preparation route of CDs/PVA film. (d) Photographs of four CDs/PVA films under λ = 365 nm UV irradiation.

(a) Excitation spectra of four CDs/PVA films. (b) The excitation spectra of C-CDs/PVA film overlapped with the absorption spectra of Ace (10 μM), providing the basis for an IFE. (c) Fluorescence spectra of C-CDs/PVA film upon titration with Ace in DMF in the range of 0–480 μM (λEx = 285 nm). (d) The fluorescence lifetime of C-CDs/PVA film before and after the addition of Ace (10 μM).

(a) Absorption spectra of Fla and excitation spectra of C-CDs/PVA film. (b) The quenching degree of C-CDs/PVA film varies with the Fla concentration at different excitation wavelengths. The differential quenching effect of four CDs/PVA films under different excitations (c) at different Fla concentrations (d) at the Fla concentrations of 100 μM.

(a) Overall response pattern generated by CDs/PVA sensors array using fluorescence and absorbance channels in response to 16 PAHs (10 μM) in DMF. 2D (b) and 3D (c) LDA canonical score plot for the response of CDs/PVA array to 16 PAHs at the concentration of 10 μΜ in DMF (15 trials each).

(a) Construction of a concentration-dependent standard LDA response library. The 3D LDA canonical score plot describes the standard response to nine PAHs at a concentration range of 0–200 μM using the first three factors (representing 89.6% of the total variance). (b) Plots of discriminant factor 2 scores vs the BaA and BaP concentration. The inset shows the linear relationship in the range of 0–30 μM.

The distribution of real samples (15 trial each) in library. Samples 1–3 were prepared by adding anonymous PAH into soil sample with concentration reduced by half in turn. Samples 4–6 were prepared using the same method by adding anonymous PAH into sludge sample. Inset is the enlarged LDA canonical score plots of soil and sludge sample.
The test Detection results of real samples by the proposed method and HPLC method.

head.ce:title: Rapid and sensitive screening of multiple polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons by a reusable fluorescent sensor array

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Hubei University of Science and Technology, Xianning 437100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Hubei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Hubei University of Science and Technology, Xianning, 437100, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan, 430074, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biogeology and Environmental Geology, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biogeology and Environmental Geology, China University of Geosciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Biogeology and Environmental Geology, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences, Wuhan 430074, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemistry, China University of Geosciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple and rapid sensing of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) remains a great technical challenge due to their chemical stability and structural similarity. Here, a simple, sensitive and cost-effective sensing strategy is proposed to detect multiple PAHs by utilizing the inner filter effect (IFE) and a reusable fluorescent sensor array consisting of four polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite carbon quantum dots (CDs) film sensors. The CDs/PVA films have a wide and tunable excitation range, which provide sufficient spectral overlap with PAHs and ensure the efficient occurrence of IFE. Under different excitations, the diverse UV absorption capacities of PAHs resulted in diverse spectral responses, enabling a unique chemical fingerprint for each PAH. Upon multivariate pattern recognition analysis, the array rendered high-throughput discrimination and sensitive quantification of 16 priority PAHs with 100% classification accuracy and detection limit as low as 57 nM. Moreover, the rapid and accurate screening of multiple environmental samples were also realized with the results consistent with high-performance liquid chromatography. This IFE-based reusable array is readily prepared, green and feasible, which exhibits great potential in environmental analysis and brings an advanced strategy to high-throughput sensing of more pollutants with similar structures and lack of recognition sites.

ce:section.ce:para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are a class of widespread persistent organic pollutants based on fused-ring aromatic molecules, which are mainly derived from incomplete combustion or pyrolysis of organic matter, as well as various industrial and natural processes (Ravindra and Sokhi, 2008; Harrison et al., 1996). Because of their mutagenicity, carcinogenicity, toxicity, bioaccumulation and environmental persistence (Luch and Baird, 2010; Jian et al., 2004; Oanh et al., 2002), PAHs pose a continuous threat to human and environmental health. These potentially serious hazards have prompted an ongoing effort to develop effective PAHs detection methods.

At present, authoritative detection methods heavily rely on tandem gas/liquid chromatography (GC/LC) and mass spectrometry (MS) (Guillén et al., 2000; Li et al., 2006; Jouyban et al., 2020; Fazaieli et al., 2020) or high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Budzinski et al., 1997; Nirmaier et al., 1996; Tong and Karasek, 1984), but multi-stage extraction, separation, and enrichment are time-consuming and labor-intensive, and require well-trained personnel, which is not suitable for rapid and low-cost analysis. In contrast, the development of simple, rapid and cost-effective detection technology is more attractive (Wilkowska and Biziuk, 2011; Lesueur et al., 2008), however, this remains a technical obstacle due to their uncharged and non-polar features, relatively stable and similar structures and low environmental concentrations. Some successful attempts for PAHs have been reported in recent years by utilizing optical sensors (i.e. fluorescence, UV–vis, etc.) (Nsibande et al., 2019; Church et al., 2016; Sehatnia et al., 2004; Valero-Navarro et al., 2007), electrochemical sensors (Carlo et al., 2008; Ning et al., 2010; Kumar et al., 2017) and immunoassay (Spier et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2010; Li et al., 1999; Ahmad et al., 2011), which demonstrate the great progress in improving sensitivity, reducing costs, rapid detection and portability. Despite these advances, there are still two major obstacles: (1) these strategies are usually based on chemical recognition, while PAHs are chemically inactive and lack recognition sites such as active substituents or heteroatoms, the development of effective sensor is not easy; (2) similarities in electronic distribution and molecular structure hinder the development of effective recognition units or specific antibodies, resulting in the inability to distinguish closely related PAHs such as benzo[k]fluoranthene and benzo[b]fluoranthene; isomers differing only in the connection of an aromatic ring (Li et al., 2016). Consequently, the development of simple and rapid methods for multiple PAHs, especially those with similar structures and chemical properties, remains an urgent challenge.

The spring up of sensor array strategy based on multivariate pattern recognition provides a new idea for PAHs detection, which reveals superiority in discrimination of structurally and chemically similar targets (Anon, 2019; Albert et al., 2000; Liu et al., 2013). Still, the sensor array requires the design of multiple broad-response sensor units, which is a challenge for PAHs. The inner filter effect (IFE), a spontaneous fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by the absorption of excitation light or emission light by an absorber, does not require any chemical response and is an ideal choice for analytes that lack active recognition sites (Zheng et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2017; Anon 1, 2016). As typical conjugated aromatics, PAHs have significant absorption in ultraviolet-visible region and overlap with the excitation region of most fluorophores, which can be used as an efficient absorber for IFE. The quenching degree of IFE depends on absorption efficiency of absorber at a given excitation wavelength. Therefore, by selecting different excitation wavelengths, PAHs with different absorption efficiencies will theoretically lead to differentiated quenching effects, which can be further distinguished by array analysis.

Inspired by these, a simple, cost-effective and reusable sensor array consisting of four polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite carbon quantum dots (CDs) film sensors was developed for the sensitive and high-throughput sensing of multiple PAHs through IFE mechanism. The absorption capacity of PAHs is quite different under different selected excitation wavelengths, which will lead to the diverse quenching of four CDs/PVA detectors. Treated with multivariate pattern recognition analysis, these abundant and diverse responses rendered the correct identification and sensitive quantification of 16 priority PAHs listed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), even including isomers, with a detection limit as low as 57 nM. The feasibility of the array was also verified by a large number of environmental samples and the output results were consistent with HPLC. As the first report of using a reusable array to detect multiple PAHs, this IFE-based sensor array realizes sensitive, high-throughput and cost-effective sensing, which not only avoids the cumbersome analysis process of traditional instrument analysis, but also avoids the complex sensor design of traditional chemosensor analysis.

The PAHs naphthalene (Nap), fluorene (Flu), acenaphthene (Ace), phenanthrene (Phe), acenaphthylene (Acy), anthracene (Ant), chrysene (Chr), benz[a]anthracene (BaA), dibenz[a,h]anthracene (DahA), pyrene (Pry), benzo[a]pyrene (BaP), fluoranthene (Fla), benzo[b]fluoranthene (BbF), benzo[k]fluoranthene (BkF), benzo[ghi]perylene (BghiP), Indeno[1,2,3-cd]pyrene (IcdP), hexachlorobenzene (HCB), 3,3′,4,4′-tetrachlorobiphenyl (PCB-77), decachlorobiphenyl (PCB-209), 1,1-dichloro-2,2-bis(4-chlorophenyl)ethane (DDD), 1,1,1-trichloro-2,2-bis(4-chlorophenyl)ethane (DDT), 3,3′,5,5′-bromobiphenyl (PBB-80), dibenzofuran (DBF), dibenzo-p-dioxin (DBD) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), m-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, L-glutamic acid, ethylenediamine, potassium hydroxide, citric acid and polyvinyl alcohol (1788) (PVA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents and solvents were analytical grade. All water used was ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm).

The emission spectra of four CDs and four CDs/PVA films were recorded using F-7000 (HITACHI, Japan) fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra of four CDs and 16 PAHs were recorded by a Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscope of Philips CM12 was employed to observe the morphology of CDs. The actual samples and spiked anonymous samples were analyzed by HPLC (UltiMate 3000 RSLCnano, Thermo Scientific, USA). The multivariate fluorescence channels and absorption channels signals were collected through a Bio Tek Synergy II multimode microwell plate reader. The obtained data matrixes were treated with principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA) using SPSS software (version 20.0).

Four CDs (blue CDs (B-CDs); cyan-blue CDs (C-CDs); green CDs (G-CDs); yellow CDs (Y-CDs)) with different excitations and emissions were synthesized using a one-pot solvothermal method. For the synthesis of B-CDs/G-CDs (Jiang et al., 2019, 2015), 0.4 g of m-phenylenediamine/o-phenylenediamine was firstly dissolved in 40 mL of ethanol, then transferred them into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)(PTFE)-lined autoclave and maintained in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. After reaction, the cooled mixtures were purified by silica gel column chromatography using dichloromethane/methanol as eluent to obtain pure B-CDs/G-CDs with a yield of about 10 wt%.

For the synthesis of C-CDs (Zhu et al., 2013), 2.5 g of citric acid and 0.25 mL of ethylenediamine were first dissolved in 40 mL of water, Then the mixtures were transferred into a PTFE-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 7 h. After reaction, the cooled C-CDs solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag with MW = 500 for 24 h (change the water every 6 h). Finally, brown fluffy C-CDs solids could be obtained by freeze-drying.

For the synthesis of Y-CDs (Ding et al., 2018), 0.26 g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.18 g of L-glutamic acid were firstly dissolved in 40 mL of DMF, and then added into a PTFE-lined autoclave, followed by heating in an oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the collected Y-CDs solution was filtered with a filter membrane and then precipitated with potassium hydroxide. Finally, the Y-CDs precipitates were collected by centrifugal separation at 14, 000 r min−1 for 10 mins.

4.2 g of PVA was first completely dissolved into 100 mL of hot water, and then cooled it for later use. PVA composite CDs films were perpetrated according to the reported method (Jiang et al., 2015). Briefly, 200 μL of CDs solution (1 mg/mL in ethanol) was thoroughly mixed with 1900 μL of the above PVA solution, and then casted on a clean smooth glass substrate, and dried for one day at room temperature. After peeling the dry CDs/PVA film from the glass substrate, the surface of film was covered with a layer of PVA again, and then dried on the glass substrate at room temperature for one day. The collected CDs/PVA films were stored in a dry and dark place.

The QYs of four CDs were measured using rhodamine 6 G (Φr = 0.95) as the standard reference. QYs measurement of rhodamine 6 G was carried out in ethanol and four CDs were performed in DMF solution. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and C-CDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs and Y-CDs were collected at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. Absorbance intensity was recorded at excitation wavelength. To make the results more reliable, the concentrations of CDs and rhodamine 6 G needed to be adjusted to ensure that their absorbance intensities at excitation wavelength were between 0 and 0.1. Finally, the QY were calculated according to the following equation:(Jiang et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2021)
Φ
s
=
Φ
r
F
s
F
r
×
η
s
η
r
×
A
r
A
s
Where s represents samples and r represents standard reference, Φ is QYs, F is integrated area of fluorescence spectra (integrated from 410 nm to 650 nm), A represents absorbance intensity at excitation wavelength; η is solvent refractive index (ethanol, 1.36; DMF, 1.43).

All spectroscopic tests were performed at 25 ℃. The test solvent was DMF solution. For PAHs fluorescent titration tests and QY measurements, a piece of clean quartz glass attached with CDs/PVA film was first prepared, and then inserted into the cuvette and fixed. The following tests were performed in the same manner as conventional fluorescence analysis. The PAHs and solvent should be thoroughly mixed before each test, and then tested immediately. It should be pointed out that the clean quartz glass covered with CDs/PVA film was quite stable, and it could be tested repeatedly. In this work, each film was reused more than 50 times.

Multivariate signals were recorded by Bio Tek Synergy II multimode microwell plate reader, the absorbance signals were measured by a monochromator and the steady-state fluorescence signals were measured by bandpass filter sets. The 384-well plate is made of cycloolefin polymer with clear flat bottom, and the size is 127.7 mm (length) × 85.5 mm (width) × 14.2 mm (height). Total volume of each well is about 105 μL, and the recommended operating volume is 80 μL. For a typical multivariate signal collection process, 12 μL of PVA and CDs mixed solution (CDs, 0.125 mg/mL; PVA, 50 mg/mL) was first added to each well and dried at room temperature, and then 8 μL of PVA was added again and dried. Next, 40 μL of DMF was added to each well, followed by PAHs, and then evenly mixed. The concentration of PAHs was controlled by changing the added volume of PAHs and ensuring that the concentration of PAHs was between 0 ~ 500 μM. The signals were collected immediately by a multimode plate reader after parameter setting. The excitation wavelength was set to 280 nm, 320 nm, 350 nm, 380 nm and 410 nm in turn, and the fluorescence signals of four CDs/PVA films (B-CDs/PVA, 350–600 nm; C-CDs/PVA, 350–600 nm; G-CDs/PVA, 420–700 nm; Y-CDs/PVA, 440–700 nm) were collected at each excitation wavelength. In all multivariate signal acquisition, each sample was prepared and tested with 15 repetitions.

For actual sample analysis, 10 g of dry soil and lakebed sludge samples were extracted respectively by Soxhlet extraction method using n-hexane as extractant. The collected extracts were concentrated with a nitrogen blower and then diluted with DMF to 10 mL. The next processes were the same as above.

The detection of PAHs by IFE requires that the absorption spectra of PAHs overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophore. Here, we selected 16 priority PAHs listed by the EPA as monitoring targets ( Fig. 1a), and analyzed their absorption and emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. S1, the absorbances of 16 PAHs were concentrated in the range of 265–420 nm, and the absorbance intensity of each PAH varied with wavelengths. Many PAHs displayed inherent fluorescence due to their typical conjugate structure, and some were even strong, such as BaP, which might interfere with subsequent fluorescent detection (Fig. S2). On the basis of these spectroscopic properties, the high-efficiency IFE-based fluorescent sensors for PAHs should meet the following conditions: (1) the optimum excitation wavelength should be in the range of 265–420 nm; (2) to avoid the interference of intrinsic fluorescence, the QYs of sensors should be high enough, or the emission spectra should not be overlapped with the intrinsic emission spectra of PAHs; (3) to avoid potential interference factors that may cause quenching, the fluorescence sensor needs to be extremely stable.

Guided by these requirements, we selected CDs as fluorescent materials owing to their low-cost, easy preparation, high QYs, and tunable excitation/emission spectra. To acquire the abundant inter-reaction diversity required for a high-throughput sensor array, four high QYs CDs (bule CDs, B-CDs; cyan CDs, C-CDs; green CDs, G-CDs; yellow CDs, Y-CDs) with different excitation and emission spectra were synthesized by a solvothermal method ( Fig. 2a, Fig. S3). The obtained multi-color CDs were quite bright under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2b), with QYs up to 0.21, 0.72, 0.27, 0.23 respectively when referring to rhodamine 6 G (Fig. S4). TEM results showed that the CDs were homogeneous and well-dispersed, with average particle sizes of 1.8, 3.1, 5.2 and 7.6 nm, respectively. Clear lattice fringes with a pitch of 0.22 nm could also be clearly observed, which was attributed to the (100) lattice distance of graphitic carbon (Fig. S5).

Bare CDs were susceptible and their emissions were easily quenched by interferents such as heavy metal ions. Therefore, stable and transparent PVA was introduced as a protective shell. To prevent the possible escape of CDs on the surface of CDs/PVA film during the detection process, we further covered the CDs/PVA film with a thin PVA layer (Fig. 2c). As expected, the resulting CDs/PVA films were quite stable, which were not only immune to common high-concentration interferents, but also could be stably stored without any emission decrease (Fig. S6). Moreover, the coverage of double-layer PVA films did not significantly reduce the emission intensity of CDs (Fig. S7). These ultra-stable fluorescent films eliminate most of the factors that may cause quenching and ensure the accuracy of IFE detection.

The excitation ranges of four films were 250–375 nm, 250–400 nm, 275–450 nm and 280–475 nm respectively, which perfectly overlapped with the absorption spectra of 16 selected PAHs ( Fig. 3a), providing the basis for IFE detection of PAHs. Here, Ace was selected as an example to verify the feasibility of using CDs/PVA films to detect PAHs by IFE. Fig. 3b showed that Ace had a strong absorption peak at 285 nm, which was located in the spectral overlap area. When excited at 285 nm, the emission of C-CDs/PVA decreased significantly with the increase of Ace concentration, and was almost completely quenched when increasing to 480 μM (Fig. 3c), showing a typical IFE phenomenon. However, due to the significant spectral overlap, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may also be a potential quenching cause. We then measured the fluorescence lifetime of C-CDs/PVA film before and after the addition of Ace. Fig. 3d showed that the fluorescence lifetime was unchanged upon the addition of Ace, indicating the quenching was a static quenching mechanism, which ruled out the FRET that belonged to dynamic quenching (Chen et al., 2016; Min et al., 2016). Given the stability and undisturbedness of CDs/PVA film, it can be concluded that IFE is the only reasonable quenching mechanism.

The strong stability of CDs/PVA films and the IFE property that is free from chemical response make the film reusable. Reusability analysis showed that four films could output stable quenching results after being reused for at least 100 times within two weeks (Fig. S8). This excellent reusability is important from the perspective of green chemistry. Combined with the simple preparation of CDs/PVA films without the usage of expensive and toxic reagents, our strategy is environmentally friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diversified response of film sensors to PAHs
ce:section.ce:para: Optimal detection conditions are first evaluated. As a detection technique for environmental analysis, the selection of hydrophilic detection solvent is necessary. However, many PAHs have non-polar structures and are easily soluble in hydrophobic solvents such as benzene, toluene, and xylene, and have poor solubility in many hydrophilic solvents such as water and ethyl alcohol. We give priority to four commonly used water-soluble solvents: DMF, acetonitrile, THF and DMSO. The results showed that DMF had better solubility and detection effect for 16 PAHs, so DMF was selected as the detection solvent (Fig. S9). Optimal detection temperature was also evaluated, the results showed that the temperature had little effect on the detection effect (Fig. S10), so 25 ℃ was selected as the detection temperature.

IFE-induced quenching is determined by the absorbance efficiency of PAHs which varies with species and wavelengths. By selecting different excitation wavelengths, the diverse absorbances at corresponding excitations will inevitably lead to the differentiated quenching results, which can be used for array discrimination. Here, we first verify the diverse quenching effect of 16 PAHs on film sensor by selecting 280, 320, 350, 380, 410 nm in the overlapping region of PAHs and CDs/PVA spectra as excitation wavelengths ( Fig. 4a) and collecting the maximum emission intensities of CDs/PVA films (B-CDs/PVA, 430 nm; C-CDs/PVA, 465 nm; G-CDs/PVA, 515 nm; Y-CDs/PVA, 540 nm). Fig. 4b summarized the quenching results of C-CDs/PVA titration with Fla as an example. The results showed that the quenching effect varied with the absorption intensity at the excitation wavelength. The stronger the absorbance was, the more obvious the quenching could be observed. In addition, other 15 PAHs also showed similar results, the quenching effect of C-CDs/PVA was significantly affected by absorption efficiency (Fig. S11-Fig. S26). Such a typical absorption-related quenching effect lays the foundation for the array output abundant differential signal.

Next, we further evaluated the impact of four CDs/PVA film sensors with different excitation spectra on the diverse response of PAHs. As shown in Fig. 4c, the quenching of four films showed significant absorption dependence, which increased with the increase of absorption intensity at the excitation wavelength. Moreover, subtle but noticeable differences in quenching effects could also be observed at a given excitation wavelength, which could be clearly observed in Fig. 4d, demonstrating that tiny excitation spectra adjustments promoted the diverse signal output. This phenomenon is mainly caused by the different optimal excitation wavelengths of four films, the closer the selected excitation wavelength is to the optimal excitation wavelength, the higher the quenching efficiency will be. Typical IFE quenching characteristic that depends on absorption efficiency combined with minor excitation spectra variations between four films, ensuring an abundant and diverse signal output, which is suitable for identification.

Encouraged by these abundant and diverse responses, we constructed a fluorescence sensor array composed of four CDs/PVA film sensors to discriminate 16 PAHs through IFE. To maximize the distinction of signals, the response signals were recorded from 20 fluorescent channels (4 sensors × 5 excitation wavelengths) and 8 absorbance channels. The fluorescent signals were collected at the excitation wavelength of 280, 320, 350, 380 and 410, which covered the spectral overlap areas. The absorption signals were recorded from 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400 and 420 nm, which covered the main absorbance range of PAHs. Fig. 5a displayed an overall response pattern of the sensor array to 16 PAHs with a concentration of 10 μM after collecting the data from 28 channels. It could be clearly observed that the response of each PAH was unique and distinguishable, indicating that by compiling the 28-channel data of the CDs/PVA sensor array, a unique multi-dimensional response pattern matrix could be created and applied to high-throughput pattern recognition research.

To evaluate the recognition ability of the array, 16 PAHs with a concentration of 10 μM were selected as analytes. Each PAH-CDs/PVA combination was prepared in 15 replicates by reusing the same film, and all multivariant signals were recorded from 28 channels using microwell plate reader. The obtained training matrix (28 channels×16 PAHs×15 replicates) was then treated with linear discriminant analysis (LDA). As shown in Fig. 5b, significant distinction of 16 PAHs was observed in two-dimensional (2D) LDA canonical scores plot when only using the first two factors (representing 62% of the total information). The same species of PAHs with different replicates were closely clustered (Fig. 5b, inset), demonstrating the signal robustness of the array. While different species of PAHs were well-discrete, even hard-to-distinguish isomers such as Ant and Phe, BaA and Chr, BkF and BbF could also be clearly classified.

To retain more discriminative information, the third factor was introduced to present the outputs in the form of a three-dimensional (3D) diagram. As displayed in Fig. 5c, a better dispersion for 16 PAHs could be observed in 3D LDA plot (representing 74.4% of the total variance). Nap, Flu, and Phe that were visually relatively stacked in the 2D plot were further distinguished in the 3D LDA plot. Moreover, by collecting the canonical score of first five factors (representing 89.6% of the total variance) and drawing the LDA standard scores plot with any two factors, a clear and intuitive classification result could also be obtained (Fig. S27). Similarly, the accurate classification of 16 PAHs with other concentrations was also obtained (Fig. S28). The cross-validation procedure showed a classification accuracy of 100% for all PAHs (Table S1). These satisfactory discrimination results fully demonstrate the powerful discriminative ability of the array.

We further analyzed the relative contribution of each channel to first five discrimination factors through LDA output component matrix (Table S2). The results showed that 20 fluorescent channels had well-proportioned contributions, and there were neither absolute dominant contributors nor non-contribution channels, reflecting the significant cross-reactivity between the sensors and PAHs. The 8 absorbance channels also made an important contribution, accounting for a quarter of the total contribution. Therefore, the strong discriminatory power of the array is attributed to the synergistic effect of cross-reactivity and the introduction of absorbance channels, where cross-reactivity comes from tunable excitation spectra and IFE quenching characteristic that depends on absorbance efficiency.

In addition to LDA, the multidimensional response signals were further investigated by principal component analysis (PCA) to evaluate the discrimination ability. The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy and Bartlett’s test of sphericity implied that the multidimensional data were suitable for PCA with a KMO value of 0.77 and a Sig. value of 0.00. Similar to LDA, a clear classification of 16 PAHs with intra-cluster aggregation and inter-cluster dispersion was obtained by using the first three principal components (PCs) (representing 77.9% of the total variance), which once again confirmed the powerful discriminative ability (Fig. S29). The contribution analysis of each channel to the first five PCs through the component matrix (representing 93.47% of the total variance) showed that all channels had well-proportioned contributions, verifying the cross-reactivity of the array again (Table S3).

The selectivity of the array was evaluated in the presence of 8 other types of aromatic persistent organic pollutants, including organochlorine pesticides (DDD, DDT), polychlorinated biphenyls (PCB-77, PCB-209), polybrominated biphenyls (PBB-80), hexachlorobenzene (HCB), dioxins and furans (DBD, DBF). As displayed in Fig. S30, the distributions of HCB, PCB-77, PCB-209, PBB-80 tightly surrounded the control points, indicating that the array was almost unresponsive to them. In contrast, the responses of array to DDT, DDB, DBD and DBF were slightly stronger, but far weaker than that of PAHs, and their clusters were clearly separated from PAHs, indicating that the array had better selectivity to PAHs.

The above discussions have demonstrated the excellent discriminative ability of sensor array, and here we will demonstrate its potential in rapid quantification of multiple PAHs. For the quantitative analysis, a concentration-dependent standard response library was first established by collecting standard distributions of 16 PAHs in LDA canonical scores plot at the concentration range of 0 ~ 200 μM (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 160 and 200 μM). To present the standard response library in a clear and intuitive manner, we prioritized the standard distributions of 9 more harmful PAHs and showed them in Fig. 6a, more details about the standard distributions of 16 PAHs were presented in Fig. S31.

Fig. 6a displayed the overall responses consisting of average LDA scores for each concentration gradient of the selected PAHs. Each point was the centroid of 15 parallel test data, representing the standard distribution of specified PAH at corresponding concentration. Distribution bands consisting of the same color data points represented continuous spatial variation of the given PAH in the concentration range of 0–200 μM. All distribution bands originated from the same location (control, 0 μM) and then gradually extended in different directions as concentration increased without overlapping each other. This multi-directional extension with increasing concentration could also be clearly observed in 2D LDA plot (Fig. S32). As a result, a and high-dispersion PAHs standard LDA response library with intra-cluster aggregation and inter-cluster dispersion was established.

Based on this concentration-dependent PAHs rough database, it is possible to quantify PAHs. As shown in Fig. S33, just as the position of PAHs in response library extended in an orderly manner as the concentration increases, their scores in different factors also showed regular changes as the concentration increases, which could be used for quantification. Here, BaP, one of the world-recognized three most strong carcinogens, and BaA were selected as representative examples to expound the quantification processes. As shown in Fig. 6b, the factor 2 scores of BaA and BaP displayed an overall saturation tendency, but exhibited linear changes in low concentration interval (Fig. 6b, inset). Using the 3σ/k rule (σ is the standard deviation of blank response, and k is the slope of calibration curve), the detection limits for BaP and BaA were calculated to be 57 nM and 219 nM. Using the same method, the rest of PAHs could also be sensitively quantified (Fig. S34). We attempted to compare the detection performance of this array with similar fluorescent arrays for PAHs, but only a few cases were found. Compared with them, our array is simpler, more efficient and more sensitive, and had the advantage of reusability (Table S4) (Anon, 2019; Doong et al., 2005; Serio et al., 2015; Discenza et al., 2018).

The crucial goal of constructing such a concentration-dependent LDA standard response database is to demonstrate that by using such a film-based sensor array, it is possible to efficiently identify and quantify unknown PAHs. In theory, the location of sample in database can not only indicate its category, but also calculate its concentration according to the corresponding scores, which provides a basis for rapid screening of unknown samples. To verify our assumption, we first prepared two actual samples by collecting soil and lakebed sludge, and then prepared two sets of unknown samples by randomly adding two anonymous PAHs with unknown concentrations into them, and named sample 1 and sample 4. The two samples were then diluted to one-half (named sample 2 and sample 5) and one-fourth (named sample 3 and sample 6) of their initial concentrations. A total of 15 replicates were prepared for each sample-sensor combination and analyzed with the proposed array using the same procedure as above. The collected response signals were combined with the standard response data and then injected into LDA.

The distributions of real samples in LDA plot were displayed in Fig. 7, and the details were shown in Fig. S35. The locations of soil and sludge samples were closely clustered around the initial position (control, 0 μM), indicating that no PAHs were contained, which was further confirmed by HPLC analysis. Samples 1–3 with multiple concentration were closely distributed around the BaP distribution zone, indicating that they belonged to the same category as BaP. Their positions not only directly clarify their category, but also imply their concentrations. Using the linear relationship established between the scores of factor 2 and the concentration (Fig. S36), the concentrations of samples 1–3 were calculated to be 57.23, 26.48 and 14.37 μM ( Table 1), which basically verified their multiple relationship. These results were further verified by HPLC, and the results showed that they were basically consistent with the HPLC. Similarly, the tight aggregation of samples 4–6 around the Pyr distribution band indicated that they were classified as Pyr. The corresponding concentrations were calculated to be 87.73, 46.58 and 21.23 μM, which also agreed with the HPLC results. Therefore, based on the dose-dependent LDA standard response diagram, we have successfully realized the rapid quantification and identification of actual PAHs samples.

In summary, we have proposed a straightforward approach for rapid screening of multiple PAHs by utilizing IFE and fluorescent sensor array-based sensing. The synergistic effect of the tunable excitation wavelength of CDs/PVA film sensors and the IFE quenching characteristic that relies on absorber absorption efficiency renders the array significant cross-reactivity, allowing the generation of unique chemical fingerprint for each PAH. Treated with LDA and PCA, 16 priority PAHs could be accurately identified and sensitively quantified with an accuracy of 100% and a detection limit as low as 57 nM. Moreover, the sensor array exhibited excellent anti-interference capability in complex environments, enabling rapid and accurate screening of multiple actual environmental samples, with the output results consistent with HPLC. Besides, our array is readily prepared, cost-effective and reusable, which is of great importance from the perspective of green chemistry. Such a simple and high-throughput IFE-based array strategy makes up for the complicated operation and time consumption of traditional PAHs analysis, and is expected to be expanded to identify more similar species that lack active recognition sites.

Linfeng Chen: Investigation, Visualization, Data Curation, Writing – original draft. Xike Tian: Data Curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yong Li: Methodology, Data curation. Chao Yang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yunjie Huang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yulun Nie: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
